titlestmt.titleproper: Beckham (Stephen Dow) collection of student activism ephemera
Finding Aid for the Stephen Dow Beckham collection of student activism ephemera UARC.0820
titlestmt.author: Finding aid prepared by Kelly Besser, 2025.

p.date: Online finding aid last updated 2026 March 23.

address.addressline: Room A1713, Charles E. Young Research Library

publicationstmt.p: Finding aid description and metadata in this record are licensed under a Creative Commons Public Domain Designation (CC0 1.0 Universal). See LSC's Metadata Rights Statement for more information.

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2026-03-23 14:09:00 -0700.

did.unittitle: Stephen Dow Beckham collection of student activism ephemera
did.abstract: Professor Stephen Dow Beckham arrived at UCLA in August 1964 to start graduate studies in History and Anthropology. At UCLA, he earned a MA, History/Anthropology (1966) and a PhD, History/Anthropology with "Highest Honors" (1969). This collection of ephemera documents student activism at UCLA in the mid-1960s and includes postings, publications, flyers, brochures, bumper stickers, buttons, clippings, and photographs.
did.physloc: Stored off-site. All requests to access special collections material must be made in advance using the request button located on this page.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Open for research. All requests to access special collections materials must be made in advance using the request button located on this page.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Reproduction and Use
userestrict.p: Copyright to portions of this collection has been assigned to the UCLA Library Special Collections. The library can grant permission to publish for materials to which it holds the copyright. Commercial use may require additional rights that must be determined and obtained by the researcher. All requests for copyright permission to publish must be submitted in writing to Library Special Collections. Credit shall be given as follows: The Regents of the University of California on behalf of the UCLA Library Special Collections.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: [Identification of item], Stephen Dow Beckham collection of student activism ephemera (UARC 0820). UCLA Library Special Collections, University Archives, University of California, Los Angeles.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived user interest and research value, availability of staff and resources, and competing priorities. Library Special Collections provides a standard level of preservation and access for all collections and, when time and resources permit, conducts more intensive processing. These materials have been arranged and described according to national and local standards and best practices.

We are committed to providing ethical, inclusive, and anti-racist description of the materials we steward, and to remediating existing description of our materials that contains language that may be offensive or cause harm. We invite you to submit feedback about how our collections are described, and how they could be described more accurately, by filling out the form located on our website: Report Problematic Content and Description in UCLA's Library Collections and Archives.

bioghist.head: Administrative/Biographical History
bioghist.p: Stephen Dow Beckham arrived at UCLA in August 1964 to start graduate studies in History and Anthropology. Within three weeks he was named Editorial Assistant to Dr. John Walton Caughey, Professor of History and editor of the Pacific Historical Review. In 1966 he completed his M.A. and published his thesis, Requiem for a People: The Rogue Indians and the Frontiersmen (University of Oklahoma Press, 1972).

In 1966 he was approached by an agent of the F.B.I. who offered to hire him to secure information on campus "radical groups," including identifying their leaders, objectives, and actions. This triggered his publication of the opinion essay, "Free Inquiry and Free Expression at UCLA - How Safe?" (Folder 6: UCLA Daily Bruin, 1966 May 6). The F.B.I. also sought to use R.O.T.C. cadets to secure information on campus activity.

In June 1969, he and his wife Patti moved to Oregon where he was named Assistant Professor of History at Linfield College. That summer he completed writing his dissertation, a biography of George Gibbs (1815-1872) and flew to Los Angeles to successfully defend his research.

Stephen Dow Beckham taught college students for forty-two years. In 1977 he was hired by Lewis & Clark College where he taught in the Department of History, the Master of Arts in Teaching program, and Indian Law seminars. He retired in 2012 as the Pamplin Professor of History, Lewis & Clark College.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Content
scopecontent.p: This collection of ephemera documents student activism at UCLA in the mid-1960s and includes postings, publications, flyers, brochures, bumper stickers, buttons, clippings, and photographs. Issues documented within this collection include the following: Free Speech Movement erupting in September 1964 at UC Berkeley, resignation of Clark Kerr, President of the University of California in March 1965, anti-Vietnam War protests, Civil Rights Movement, efforts to organize UC teaching and research assistants, and special advocacy issues such as free love, anti-communism, and art protests. These materials document teach-ins and campus protests that revolved around a small platform outside Kerckhoff Hall and the student union.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This collection's arrangement reflects its existing order.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: UCLA Students. Student Activism materials (UARC.0259). Available at UCLA Library Special Collections, University Archives, University of California, Los Angeles.
John Walton Caughey Papers (Collection 998). Available at UCLA Library Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.

did.unittitle: Flyer, posting, and UCLA Free Speech Movement newsletter
W.E.B. DuBois Club flyers, newsletters, clippings, and bumper stickers
Flyers, postings, clippings, cartoons, and publications
